SYMPTOM: pitch tubes, boring dust, emergence holes, crown fade/red needles
HOSTS_ALL: Most western pines; especially lodgepole, ponderosa, whitebark, limber, sugar, western white, Jeffrey
SEIN_knownhosts: Lodgepole, whitebark pine, foxtail pine, limber pine, and western white pine
DESCRIPTION: Look for obvious pitch tubes on the bole. Trees may exhibit crown fade. Diagnostic galleries beneath the bark may help confirm identification after mortality. SEVERITY: 1 – Current attack: foliage green, boring dust, pitching. 2 – Prior year attack: fading foliage (some green somewhere on crown), boring dust, pitching. 3 – Top kill (no green needles remaining) possibly with green needles on lower live portion.

DESCRIPTION: Look for round to pear-shaped woody swellings (galls) on branches or stems. Bright orange sporulation may occur in spring and early summer. Large stem infections may develop flared "hip" cankers. Unlike white pine blister rust, infections form discrete galls rather than elongated cankers and do not require an alternate host.

HOSTS_ALL: White fir, red fir, grand fir, subalpine fir
DESCRIPTION: Look for crown fade or topkill in Abies trees. Small round emergence holes, pitch tubes, and boring dust may be present. If the tree is deceased, maternal galleries typically run horizontally and are intersected by larval mines at roughly perpendicular angles.

SYMPTOM: large pitch tubes, lower bole emergence holes, granular boring dust
SEIN_knownhosts: Lodgepole, whitebark pine, foxtail pine, limber pine, and western white pine
DESCRIPTION: Look for large, light pink to reddish-brown pitch tubes concentrated near the base of the tree, rarely occurring more than 8 ft (2.4 m) above the ground. White granular boring dust may accumulate around the base of attacked trees. Large pitch tubes, galleries, and adult size distinguish red turpentine beetle from other bark beetles.

SYMPTOM: emergence holes, bark cracking, crown fade
DESCRIPTION: Primarily attacks drought stressed Abies, may also attack healthy trees. On living trees, look for fading needles and oval- to D-shaped emergence holes (3–6 mm wide). Pitch tubes and boring dust are generally absent. For dead trees, look for broad, winding galleries packed with frass beneath the bark. Larvae have a distinctive body shape.

SYMPTOM: swelling, witches' brooms, branch mortality, odd bending
SEIN_knownhosts: Hemlock, lodgepole, whitebark pine, foxtail pine, limber pine, and western white pine
DESCRIPTION: A broad pest genus with many possible hosts; pines, hemlock, and fir are susceptible, though the responsible mistletoe species differs. Look for abnormal brooming, branch distortion, and orange-to-green fruiting bodies reminiscent of coral emerging from branches or boles. Infection may cause branch death, top-kill, and weak or distorted ("bendy") wood. Severe infections can produce dramatic stem deformities, particularly in smaller trees.

A bark beetle associated with Douglas-fir and hemlock. Look for top-kill, branch-kill, or mortality in small-diameter trees and the upper portions of larger trees. If the tree is deceased, bark removal may reveal short longitudinal galleries, often branching upward and downward from a central chamber. Gallery patterns and emergence holes help distinguish this species.

SEIN_knownhosts: Whitebark pine, foxtail pine, limber pine, and western white pine (no lodgepole?)
DESCRIPTION: Look for pitching, swelling, discoloration, and roughened bark at the canker site, possibly with distal branch flagging. Animal chewing is common around active cankers. Pale orange fungal structures may erupt through the bark.

HOSTS_ALL: Most conifers; commonly pines, spruce, Douglas-fir
SEIN_knownhosts: Lodgepole, whitebark pine, foxtail pine, limber pine, and western white pine
DESCRIPTION: A varied pest genus with many possible hosts. Adults have a distinctive scooped-out hind end edged with small spines. Look for small round emergence holes, boring dust, and occasional pitch tubes. Flagging may occur, particularly in upper portions of the crown. If the tree is deceased, galleries often have characteristic I-, Y-, or X-shape. Gallery appearance depends on host traits: in pines, sawdust and frass packing is more common; in spruce, galleries will likely be more compact and "messier"
Look for crown fade and needle drop. Hold yellowed needles up to sunlight to look for a silhouette of the larvae or frass inside. Older mined needles will have small exit holes.

SYMPTOM: pitch masses, low emergence holes, branch deformity
DESCRIPTION:
Look for obvious large pitch masses and streamers (creamy-colored oozing pitch masses).
SEVERITY:
1 – Current/emerging: foliage still green; pitching present.
2 – Prior year: fading foliage (some green needles remain in the crown); pitch masses or pitching present.
3 – Top or branch kill: tree or branch dead distal to pitch streamers (no green needles remain); green needles may persist on the lower live portion of the tree.

SEVERITY: 1 – White, fluffy aphid secretion observed at base of some needle bundles. 2 – White, fluffy aphid secretion observed at base of many needle bundles and/or portions of bark on branches/bole. 3 – White, fluffy aphid secretion observed covering ~25% or more of the tree’s surface area.

SEIN_knownhosts: Lodgepole, whitebark pine, foxtail pine, limber pine, and western white pine
DESCRIPTION: Look for tiny reddish pitch tubes on small branches, often on the underside. Heavy infestations may girdle branches.

Look for fading branch tips and distinctive hollow pitch nodules at feeding sites. Pitch nodules are smooth, round to elongate, approximately 10–25 mm long, and may appear light purple or red. Terminal leaders are occasionally damaged, potentially resulting in multiple leaders. Heavy infestations may occur in localized areas

SYMPTOM: boring dust, pitch streaming, emergence holes
DESCRIPTION: Look for reddish-orange frass in bark crevices, though frass may wash away and attacks may occur above eye level. Unlike many bark beetles, pitch tubes are often absent; instead, pitch may stream down the bole from the the colonized area. Trees may later exhibit crown fade and reddening of the foliage.

A bark beetle associated with Douglas-fir and hemlock. Look for top-kill, branch-kill, or mortality in small-diameter trees and the upper portions of larger trees. If the tree is deceased, bark removal may reveal short longitudinal galleries, often branching upward and downward from a central chamber. Gallery patterns and emergence holes help distinguish this species from other Douglas-fir bark beetles. elaborate...

If the tree is deceased, bark removal may reveal short longitudinal galleries, often branching upward and downward from a central chamber. Look for reddish-brown, shriveled needles beginning on current year's growth. Defoliation often progresses from the upper crown and outer branches downward and inward as larvae mature. Older larvae have distinctive tussocks along the back. Adult females are flightless and may be observed near egg masses, with rudimentary wings and a large abdomen. Egg masses are laid on the cocoon from which the female emerged.
